---
title: "Week 8 Milestone Analysis"
author: "<img src='images/logo.png' width='50' /> <br> Dairy Cow Foot Doc"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
format:
  html:
    page-layout: full
    theme: flatly
    df_print: paged
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: false
    code-fold: true
execute:
  message: false
  warning: false
  echo: false
embed-resources: true
params:
  event:
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

if (!require("pacman")) install.packages("pacman")
pacman::p_load(
  broom,
  tidyverse,
  dtplyr,
  gt,
  arrow,
  rmarkdown,
  lubridate,
  quarto, 
  arrow,
  survival,
  survminer,
  ggsurvfit,
  flextable,
  gtsummary,
  marginaleffects,
  scales,
  zoo
  )

events_formatted <- read_parquet(
  '../data/intermediate_files/events_formatted.parquet') 

animals <- read_parquet(
  '../data/intermediate_files/animals.parquet')

  
# load GC's functions
source(here::here('functions/fxn_load_os_fxns.R'))
fxn_load_os_fxns()


# create variables for inline code
farm_name <- unique(events_formatted$location_event)

date_max_pull <- max(events_formatted$date_event, na.rm = TRUE)
date_min_pull <- date_max_pull - years(1)

# pull solution data
source(here::here('functions/fxn_milestone8_solution.R'))

```

```{r}

# lame data test new function
# 
all_lesions <- c("dd", "footrot", 'wld', "sole_ulcer","injury", 
                             "cork", "other", "hemorrhage",
                             "sole_fracture", "toe_ulcer", "thin",
                             "inf", "noninf", "toe", "axial", "lesion")

diseases <- c("inf", "noninf", "lesion")

lame_data <- events_formatted |> 
  select(id_animal, date_birth, date_fresh,lact_number, lact_group, 
         date_archived, date_event, event, 
         remark_letters1, protocols_letters1, location_event) |> 
  filter(event %in% c("LAME", "TRIM")) |>
  fxn_code_lesions(protocol_var = protocols_letters1,
                  remark_var = remark_letters1) |> 
  # fix ab and NA issues
  filter(!is.na(trimonly)) |>
  mutate(noninf = case_when(
    other == 1 & str_detect(protocols_letters1, "Ab") ~ 1,
    .default = noninf),
    # remove other catergory if abscess is found
    other = case_when(
      other == 1 & str_detect(protocols_letters1, "Ab") ~ 0,
      .default = other)
    ) |> 
  fxn_collapse_lesions(lesions = all_lesions) |> 
  fxn_trim_vars(location_event, id_animal,
                date_var = date_event,
                trimonly_var = trimonly) |> 
  fxn_dz_status(disease_cols = diseases,
                event_filter = trimonly == 0) |> 
# join with animals to get left date
  left_join(animals |> 
              select(id_animal, date_sold, date_died, date_left),
            by = "id_animal") |> 
# add cull data
  mutate(
    # create culling variables
    culled_ever = if_else(is.na(date_left), 0, 1),
    days_to_cull = date_left - date_event,
    # temp censor date need to adjust
    date_censor = ymd(date_max_pull) 
  ) |> 
  mutate(days_to_censor_cull = case_when(culled_ever == 1 ~
                                     days_to_cull,
                                    # cows not culled
                                    culled_ever == 0 ~
                                      date_censor - 
                                                    date_event,
                                    .default =  NA
                                    ),
    across(starts_with("days"), as.numeric)
    ) |> 
  group_by(location_event, id_animal) |> 
  mutate(culled_last_trim = 
           if_else(culled_ever == 1 & !is.na(days_to_next_trim), 0, 
                   culled_ever),
         culled_30 = if_else(culled_ever == 1 & 
                               days_to_cull <=30, 1, 0
                             )
         )|>
  ungroup() |> 
  select(location_event, id_animal, date_birth, lact_number, lact_group,
         date_event,
         date_left, date_died, date_sold,
         lesion, inf, noninf, trimonly, times_trimmed, date_censor, 
         days_to_cull, days_to_censor_cull,culled_ever, culled_last_trim,
         culled_30, date_prev_trim, days_to_next_trim, 
         starts_with("life_"), starts_with("status_")
         )

# 
#   filter(lact>0) |>
#   filter(culled_last_trim == 1) |> 
#   filter(date_event >= date_max_pull - months(12))





```

```{r}
#| label: functions to hide my code
#| include: false

solutions_violin <- function () {
  
violin <- log_data |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = days_to_cull, y = fct_rev(lesion),
             fill = lesion)
                  ) +
  geom_violin(draw_quantiles = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75),
              quantile.colour = "red",
              scale = "count")+
  theme_minimal()+
  labs(x = "Days to Culling after Trim",
       y = "")+
  scale_fill_viridis_d()+
  theme(legend.position = "none")


violin

}

# heat map of culled 30 with denominator # cows trimmed

solutions_heatmap <- function () {
heat_map_lact <- log_data |>
  group_by(noninf, lact_group, culled_30) |> 
  summarise(count = n(), .groups = "drop") |> 
  group_by(noninf, lact_group) |>
  mutate(pct = count / sum(count)) |> 
  ungroup()

heat_map_sum <- log_data |> 
  group_by(noninf, culled_30) |>
  summarise(count = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  group_by(noninf) |>
  mutate(pct = count / sum(count),
         lact_group = "Overall") |> 
  ungroup()


heat_map <- bind_rows(heat_map_lact, heat_map_sum)
 

heat_plot <- heat_map |> 
  filter(culled_30 == 1) |>
  mutate(lact_group = fct_relevel(lact_group, 
                                  c("LACT 3+", 
                                    "LACT 2", 
                                    "LACT 1", 
                                    "Overall"))                                    ) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = noninf, y = fct_rev(lact_group),
             fill = pct)
         ) +
  geom_tile()+
  geom_text(aes(label = str_glue(
    "{round(pct * 100, 1)}% ({count})")),
          color = "black", 
          size = 3)+
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "% Culled within 30 days",
                       labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                       alpha = 0.8,
                       option = "C")+
  theme_minimal()+
  labs(x = "",
      y = "")+
  theme(axis.ticks.y=element_blank())

heat_plot
}

# logistic regression

solutions_logistic <- function () {

log_model <- glm(culled_30 ~ as_factor(noninf), 
    family = "binomial", data = log_data)

}


```

```{=html}
<!-- The objective of this milestone is to build on the report and functions you used last milestone to analyze culling data in 2 different ways. You will create the culling variables and then visualize the data. You will then use both logistic regression and survival analysis to explore the data. 
A few notes:  1) The logic is to create culling variables can be complex depending on the questions I have tried to be explicit in how I created the data set you should be using. 2)Any data (including farm name) that is reported in the text should use an inline text code chunk -->
```

# Report for `r farm_name`

-   This report shows data from the past year from
    **`r format(date_min_pull, "%b %d, %y")` to
    `r format(date_max_pull, "%b %d, %y")`**.

-   For the culling analysis cows were censored on
    `r format(date_max_pull, "%b %d, %y")` if they were not culled
    before this date.

## Objective

Your objective is to recreate the solution_data by doing the following
with the events_formatted and animals table.

1.  Filter the events_formatted table to only include LAME and TRIM
    events
2.  Then create new variables for lesions using the
    **fxn_code_lesions()** function that you used from Github last
    milestone
    -   This function is automatically loaded for you already.
    -   use (protocol_var = protocols_letters1, remark_var =
        remark_letters1) inside the function
3.  Address the abscess warning in the function by coding anything with
    ab in remark_letters1 or protocol_letter1 as noninf and remove them
    from other.
4.  Run the following 3 functions to process the lesion data further and
    create variables you will need to use later on.
    -   **fxn_collapse_lesions**(lesions = all_lesions)
    -   **fxn_trim_vars**(location_event, id_animal, date_var =
        date_event, trimonly_var = trimonly)
    -   **fxn_dz_status**(disease_cols = diseases, event_filter =
        trimonly == 0, gap = 1)
        -   ***NOTE**: All the other functions are specific for lameness
            this one is **NOT** and can be used for other diseases!*
5.  Then join this data table with the animals table to get the
    date_left, date_sold and date_died variables
6.  Then create new culling variables as described below
    -   **date_censor :** this is the last date the cow would have been
        eligible to be culled. In this dataset this would be the last date any event was entered in the data set
    -   **days_to_cull:** days from any event(Trim or Lame ) to culling
    -   **days_to_censor_cull:** This combines the above 2 items to calculate days from the event of interest (trim/lame) to culling or censor_date
        if not culled
    -   **culled_ever:** For all rows, 1 if the cow was culled, 0 if not culled
    -   **culled_last_trim:** For each row/cow, 1 if the cow was culled after that trim without being in the chute again. 0 if not culled after that trim or if she was trimmed again
    -   **culled_30**: 1 if the cow was culled \<=30 days after her trim/lame event 0 if not
7.  Visualize this culling data using both a violin plot and heat map
8.  Create some summary statistics of this data using both logistic
    regression and compare that the provided survival analysis functions

## Your Code Creation

-   The code block has some hints to help you create the solution_data
    data frame.

```{r}
#| label: your code
#| echo: TRUE

# put your code here to create the required data frame.
# name it solution_data

# you will need to use these functions and vectors with these arguments at some point in your code.

all_lesions <- c("dd", "footrot", 'wld', "sole_ulcer","injury", 
                             "cork", "other", "hemorrhage",
                             "sole_fracture", "toe_ulcer", "thin",
                             "inf", "noninf", "toe", "axial", "lesion")

diseases <- c("inf", "noninf", "lesion")

# solution_data <- 

# fxn_collapse_lesions(lesions = all_lesions) |> 
#   fxn_trim_vars(location_event, id_animal,
#                 date_var = date_event,
#                 trimonly_var = trimonly) |> 
#   fxn_dz_status(disease_cols = diseases,
#                 event_filter = trimonly == 0)


```

```{r}
#| label: create-solution-data

solution_data <- lame_data
 
waldo::compare(solution_data, solution)



```

## Visualize Data

### Violin plot

Then on the solution_data table.

-   Create a violin graph that shows the distribution of time to culling
    for cows with any lesion.
    -   restrict data to
        -   just the past 12 months
        -   data from the cow's last trimming
        -   lactating cows only
        -   positive days_to_censor_cull numbers only
-   To compare your restricted data to the solution data use
    -   *waldo::compare(log_data, restricted_data)*

```{r}
#| label: visualize-culling
#| echo: true

# put your visualization category code here

solutions_violin()


```

### Heat Map

-   For non infectious lesions create a heat map similar to this using
    the culled_30 variable.
-   Restrict the data using the same criteria as above.

```{r}
#| label: fig-example_heat
#| echo: true

solutions_heatmap()






```

# Modelling

At times it is useful to create a model to determine if the differences
between culling risks between groups are important. Since we are
modelling culling one approach is to use logistic regression to do this.
Interpreting the outputs from these models can be harder to explain as
typically they output odds ratios which aren't commonly used by people
other then researchers/data geeks. For this reason we should turn the
model outputs back into risks.

In the section below create a logistic model that models the impact of
non_infectious lesion status on culling at 30 days. - Name the model
output log_model. \_ Ignore lactation for this model. There are ways to
account for lactation but we will keep it simple for now. - We have
provide the code to make looking at the model output and to turn it into
risks.

```{r}
#| label: fig-example_logistic
#| echo: true

log_model <- solutions_logistic()

log_model_results <- tidy(log_model, exponentiate = TRUE,
                      conf.int = TRUE) |> 
  rename(oddsratio = estimate) |> 
  mutate(
    # Format p-value to 4 decimal places
    p.value = number(p.value, accuracy = 0.00000001),
    # Optional: Format OR and CI for readability
    estimate = number(oddsratio, accuracy = 0.01),
    conf.low = number(conf.low, accuracy = 0.01),
    conf.high = number(conf.high, accuracy = 0.01)
  )

log_model_results |> 
  gt()

```

### Logistic Model Risks

```{r}
#| label: fig-example_logistic_risks
#| echo: true

log_model_risks <- avg_predictions(log_model, 
                               variables = c("noninf"))

log_model_risks <- log_model_risks |>  
  select(noninf,
    Risk = estimate,
         conf.low,
         conf.high) |> 
  mutate(
    Risk = number(Risk*100, accuracy = 0.1),
    conf.low = number(conf.low*100, accuracy = 0.1),
    conf.high = number(conf.high*100, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) |> 
  gt()

log_model_risks


```

You will notice the risks from the logistic model should be the same as
the overall row in the heat map, but now we have confidence intervals
around them to give us a sense of the uncertainty of the risk.

## Survival Analysis

One problem with using logistic regression and risks to deal with
culling is that we are likely underestimating the risk of culling since
some cows may be censored before they are culled. In our example
censoring would occur if it has not been 30 days since a cow was
trimmed. A better approach to deal with this is to use survival
analysis.

Below we have provided code to create a Kaplan Meier survival curve and
table to look at the impact of non infectious lesions on culling risk
over time. You will notice that the risk estimates are higher now that
we are accounting for censoring. A good quick read about survival
analysis can be found
[here](https://www.emilyzabor.com/survival-analysis-in-r.html#kaplan-meier-plots)

```{r}
#| label: fig-example_km
#| echo: true

# survival analysis
# km anaylsys


km_fit <- survfit2(Surv(time = days_to_censor_cull,
                event = culled_last_trim) ~ noninf, 
data = log_data)

surv_plot <- km_fit |> 
  ggsurvfit(
    type = "risk"
  )+
  add_confidence_interval()+
  add_quantile()+
  scale_ggsurvfit()+
  scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "Non-Infectious Lesion Status")+
  scale_color_viridis_d(name = "Non-Infectious Lesion Status")+
  labs(x = "Days to Culling after Trim",
       y = "Percent of Cows Culled")+
  theme_minimal()+
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

surv_plot
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-example_km_table
#| echo: true

# times are adjustable

surv_table <- km_fit |>
  tbl_survfit(
    times = c(7, 30, 60, 180),
    label = " ",
    type = "risk",
    label_header = "{time} Days"
  ) %>%
  as_flex_table() %>% # makes formatting and pdf better
  add_header_row(
    top = TRUE,
    values = c(
      "Non-Infectious Lesion Status",
      "% Culled (Confidence Interval) at",
      "",
      "", ""
    )
  ) %>%
  bold(i = 1, bold = TRUE, part = "header") %>% # bolds headers
  bold(i = 2, bold = TRUE, part = "header") %>%
  merge_at(i = 1:2, j = 1, part = "header") %>% # merges 1st row
  merge_at(i = 1, j = 2:5, part = "header") # merges top columns

surv_table


```

# Extension

For this week extension continue to create standard things you would
want to report or incorporate modeling techniques to explore the data

Ideas

-   Try the code with other diseases like mastitis or culture
    information
-   Try to use the survival code to get estimates for time to repeat or
    recheck?
